perturbation.original_text: Personal Data includes, without limitation, (i) an individual’s social security number, bank or other financial account numbers, credit or debit card numbers, driver’s license number, passport number, o ther government -issued identification numbers, biometric data, health and medical information, and data about the individual obtained through a Project; (ii) financial information, employee benefits information, education records, NU identification numbers ;
perturbation.changed_text: Personal Data includes, without limitation, (i) an individual’s social security number, bank or other financial account numbers, credit or debit card numbers, driver’s license number, passport number, o ther government -issued identification numbers, biometric data, health and medical information, and data about the individual obtained through a Project; (ii) financial information, employee benefits information, education records, NU identification numbers . Northeastern University is not required to protect an individual's social security number.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that Northeastern University is not required to protect an individual's social security number directly contradicts state data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires the implementation and maintenance of a comprehensive information security program to protect the personal information of residents of the Commonwealth. By stating that NU is not required to protect social security numbers, the modified text contradicts this legal requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 1 Definitions Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Definitions Section 1. (a) As used in this chapter, the following words shall, unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings:— ''Agency'', any agency, executive office, department, board, commission, bureau, division or authority of the commonwealth, or any of its branches, or of any political subdivision thereof. ''Breach of security'', the unauthorized acquisition or unauthorized use of unencrypted data or, encrypted electronic data and the confidential process or key that is capable of compromising the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information, maintained by a person or agency that creates a substantial risk of identity theft or fraud against a resident of the commonwealth. A good faith but unauthorized acquisition of personal information by a person or agency, or employee or agent thereof, for the lawful purposes of such person or agency, is not a breach of security unless the personal information is used in an unauthorized manner or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. ''Data'' any material upon which written, drawn, spoken, visual, or electromagnetic information or images are recorded or preserved, regardless of physical form or characteristics. ''Electronic'', relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic or similar capabilities. ''Encrypted'' transformation of data through the use of a 128–bit or higher algorithmic process into a form in which there is a low probability of assigning meaning without use of a confidential process or key, unless further defined by regulation of the department of consumer affairs and business regulatio

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that Northeastern University is not required to protect an individual's social security number. This directly contradicts Massachusetts law, which, as explained by the LLM, requires the implementation and maintenance of a comprehensive information security program to protect the personal information of residents. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 define a 'breach of security' and detail requirements for data protection, implicitly including social security numbers as personal information that should be protected under a comprehensive information security program. The modified text introduces an exception that undermines this general obligation, creating a direct and enforceable contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall be one ( 1) year from the Effective Date. This Agreement may be terminated by either pa rty upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party. Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve either party of its obligations hereunder to maintain in confidence and not to use Confidential Information received hereunder for a period of three (3) years from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall be one ( 1) year from the Effective Date. This Agreement may be terminated by either pa rty upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party. Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve either party of its obligations hereunder to maintain in confidence and not to use Confidential Information received hereunder for a period of ninety (90) days from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 90 days may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, contradicting the reasonable measures required by trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By limiting the confidentiality period to 90 days, the modified text fails to provide reasonable measures to protect the trade secrets, especially considering the potential for long-term damage caused by the disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

(2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to 90 days, which contradicts the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets under Massachusetts law. The Law Explanation states that limiting the confidentiality period to 90 days fails to provide reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, and Snippet 2 reinforces this definition. By reducing the confidentiality period significantly, the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation, as it allows for the potential disclosure or use of trade secrets after only 90 days, failing to provide adequate protection as typically expected under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Should the Recipient become aware of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Recipient agrees to promptly notify the Disclosing Party in writing and , in addition to the other rights set forth in this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall have the right to immediately terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the Recipient become aware of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Recipient agrees to notify the Disclosing Party in writing within 90 days of discovery and , in addition to the other rights set forth in this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall have the right to immediately terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period for a breach to 90 days violates data breach notification laws that require timely notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires notification to affected individuals and the Attorney General 'as expediently as possible and without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach of security. The 90 day notification period is an unreasonable delay that violates the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 3 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 3 Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 3: Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3. (a) A person or agency that maintains or stores, but does not own or license data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has reason to know of a breach of security or (2) when the person or agency knows or has reason to know that the personal information of such resident was acquired or used by an unauthorized person or used for an unauthorized purpose, to the owner or licensor in accordance with this chapter. In addition to providing notice as provided herein, such person or agency shall cooperate with the owner or licensor of such information. Such cooperation shall include, but not be limited to, informing the owner or licensor of the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use, the date or approximate date of such incident and the nature thereof, and any steps the person or agency has taken or plans to take relating to the incident, except that such cooperation shall not be deemed to require the disclosure of confidential business information or trade secrets, or to provide notice to a resident that may have been affected by the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use. (b) A person or agency that owns or licenses data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has rea

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day notification period for breaches, which contradicts Massachusetts law. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 state that notification should be provided "as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay." A 90-day delay is likely to be considered an unreasonable delay, rendering the modified contract non-compliant with the law, creating a clear legal contradiction as defined in the instructions.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain security measures for Personal Data which shall be determined at the Recipient's sole discretion. Recipient is not responsible for notifying individuals of data breaches.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that security measures are at the Recipient's sole discretion and removing the responsibility to notify individuals of data breaches violates established legal standards for data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires notification to affected individuals and the Attorney General 'as expediently as possible and without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach of security. Removing this responsibility and stating that data breach responses will be determined at the Recipient's sole discretion violate the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Massachusetts law regarding data breach notification. The original text requires security measures at least as protective as those under applicable law. The changed text gives the Recipient sole discretion over security and removes the responsibility to notify individuals of data breaches. This directly conflicts with Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93H, Section 3, which states that a person or agency owning or licensing data with personal information about a resident 'shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay' when a breach occurs. By removing this notification responsibility, the modified contract violates the mandatory notification requirement specified in the law.
